data.name: Dulce de Leche Panna Cotta
data.description: A velvety, eggless Italian custard infused with rich, caramelized dulce de leche. This five-ingredient dessert melts in the mouth, delivering a perfect balance of milky sweetness and roasted caramel notes.

refrigerator.notes: Cover tightly with plastic wrap to prevent skin formation.

freezer.notes: Freezing alters the texture of the gelatin and causes weeping when thawed.

instructions.text: Pour the cold milk into a small bowl and sprinkle the gelatin powder evenly over the surface. Let it sit undisturbed for 5 to 10 minutes to bloom and hydrate.

In a medium saucepan, combine the heavy cream, 160 grams of the dulce de leche, and vanilla extract. Heat over medium heat, whisking gently, until the mixture reaches a gentle simmer at around 80 degrees Celsius (175 degrees Fahrenheit). Do not let it boil.

doneness_cues.visual: Small bubbles form around the edges of the pan, dulce de leche is completely dissolved

instructions.text: Remove the saucepan from the heat. Scrape the bloomed gelatin and milk mixture into the hot cream. Whisk thoroughly until the gelatin is completely dissolved and the mixture is entirely smooth.

doneness_cues.visual: No visible granules of gelatin remain on the whisk or spoon

instructions.text: Pour the mixture through a fine-mesh strainer into a liquid measuring cup to catch any undissolved pieces. Divide the strained liquid evenly among 4 ramekins or serving glasses.
Let the ramekins cool to room temperature, then cover each with plastic wrap. Transfer to the refrigerator and chill for at least 4 hours, or until completely set.

doneness_cues.tactile: Surface is firm to the touch and jiggles slightly like set pudding

instructions.text: To serve, dip the bottoms of the ramekins in warm water for 5 seconds. Run a thin knife around the edges and invert onto plates. Warm the remaining dulce de leche and drizzle over the top.

troubleshooting.symptom: Panna cotta separates into two distinct layers
troubleshooting.likely_cause: Mixture was not fully emulsified or was poured into molds while too hot
troubleshooting.prevention: Cool the mixture over an ice bath while whisking until slightly thickened before pouring into molds

troubleshooting.likely_cause: Gelatin was not bloomed evenly or mixture was not hot enough to dissolve it
troubleshooting.prevention: Sprinkle gelatin evenly over cold liquid and ensure cream mixture is hot enough before combining
troubleshooting.fix: Return to low heat and whisk gently until dissolved, then strain again

chef_notes: For a flawlessly smooth texture, always strain the panna cotta mixture before pouring it into your molds to catch any stubborn undissolved gelatin granules.
Unmolding can be tricky. Dipping the ramekin in warm water just long enough to melt the outermost layer of gelatin ensures a clean release.
Using a high-quality, thick dulce de leche rather than a thin caramel syrup is crucial, as its milk solids emulsify beautifully with the heavy cream.
data.cultural_context: Panna cotta, meaning cooked cream, is a classic dessert originating from the Piedmont region of Northern Italy. This variation introduces a Latin American staple, dulce de leche, creating a modern cross-cultural dessert that replaces traditional sugar with deeply roasted milk caramel.
